# Paper 1 — Verified Empirical Results

**Dataset:** focused balanced MedQA, `data/datasets/medqa_focused_1030.json`
(1,030 questions; 206 per category; 4-option A–D; **random baseline = 25%**).
**Source:** `results/base_runs/` — 5 models × 3 temperatures × 3 runs = 45 result files.
**Generated by:** `papers/paper1/analysis/compute_tables.py` (tables) and
`make_figures.py` (figures). All values recomputed from raw per-question records.

> Note: these are the **focused 1,030 (4-option)** results. They are distinct from
> the repo's `RESULTS_SUMMARY.md`, which reports the **full 12,723-question (5-option)**
> set. Accuracies here are higher partly because of the 4-option format (25% vs 20%
> chance) and the balanced category sampling.

---

## Table 3 — Overall accuracy by model and temperature (mean of 3 runs)

| Model | t=0.0 | t=0.3 | t=0.7 | StdDev @0.0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| OLMo-3 7B | 46.18 | 44.50 | 41.10 | 0.05 |
| OLMo-3 32B | 58.64 | 57.35 | 50.87 | 0.00 |
| Qwen2.5 7B | 67.96 | 66.44 | 57.67 | 0.00 |
| Qwen2.5 14B | 68.48 | 65.89 | 59.19 | 0.05 |
| Qwen2.5 32B | **73.40** | 71.65 | 64.50 | 0.00 |

All models far exceed the 25% random baseline. Qwen2.5 dominates OLMo-3 at every
scale. Determinism at t=0.0 is near-perfect (std ≤ 0.05 pp).

## Table 4 — Per-category accuracy at t=0.0 (mean of 3 runs)

| Model | Clinical Findings | Diagnosis | Mechanism/Pathophys | Next Step/Workup | Treatment/Mgmt |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| OLMo-3 7B | 41.75 | 41.59 | 54.37 | 43.20 | 50.00 |
| OLMo-3 32B | 50.97 | 56.80 | 62.14 | 60.19 | 63.11 |
| Qwen2.5 7B | 57.28 | 65.05 | 72.33 | 70.87 | 74.27 |
| Qwen2.5 14B | 57.77 | 65.53 | 72.98 | 71.36 | 74.76 |
| Qwen2.5 32B | 66.99 | 70.87 | 76.21 | 75.73 | 77.18 |

**Key pattern:** *Clinical Findings* is the hardest category for **every** model;
*Treatment/Management* and *Mechanism/Pathophysiology* are consistently the easiest.
This ordering is hidden by aggregate accuracy and is a central motivation for the paper.

## Table 5 — Run-to-run variability and answer instability

Answer instability = % of questions where the 3 runs do **not** all predict the same letter.

| Model | t=0.0 | t=0.3 | t=0.7 |
|---|---|---|---|
| OLMo-3 7B | 0.87 | 39.51 | 67.57 |
| OLMo-3 32B | 0.00 | 31.46 | 59.90 |
| Qwen2.5 7B | 0.10 | 25.92 | 55.24 |
| Qwen2.5 14B | 0.19 | 25.83 | 53.30 |
| Qwen2.5 32B | 0.00 | 19.13 | 44.66 |

**Key pattern:** instability is ~0 at t=0.0 but rises steeply with temperature — up to
**two-thirds of questions** flip answers across runs for the weakest model at t=0.7.
Larger / stronger models are more stable at the same temperature.

## Table 6 — Majority-vote vs single-run accuracy

| Model | t=0.7 single-run | t=0.7 majority-vote | gain (pp) |
|---|---|---|---|
| OLMo-3 7B | 41.10 | 44.56 | +3.46 |
| OLMo-3 32B | 50.87 | 54.76 | +3.88 |
| Qwen2.5 7B | 57.67 | 61.07 | +3.40 |
| Qwen2.5 14B | 59.19 | 63.01 | +3.82 |
| Qwen2.5 32B | 64.50 | 68.74 | +4.24 |

**Key pattern:** majority voting over 3 stochastic samples recovers 3–4 pp at t=0.7
but never closes the gap to greedy t=0.0 — self-consistency helps but is not a fix.
Directly motivates later verification/aggregation work.

## Table 7 — Temperature sensitivity (t=0.0 → t=0.7 accuracy drop, pp)

| Model | Overall | Clinical Findings | Diagnosis | Mechanism | Next Step | Treatment |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| OLMo-3 7B | 5.08 | 3.40 | 3.56 | 8.41 | 4.53 | 5.50 |
| OLMo-3 32B | 7.77 | 5.99 | 5.99 | 8.25 | 8.25 | 10.36 |
| Qwen2.5 7B | 10.29 | 4.05 | 9.39 | 12.62 | 10.68 | 14.72 |
| Qwen2.5 14B | 9.29 | 3.40 | 5.18 | 11.17 | 15.05 | 11.65 |
| Qwen2.5 32B | 8.90 | 7.77 | 9.55 | 7.28 | 11.81 | 8.09 |

**Key pattern:** higher temperature *monotonically degrades* accuracy for all models.
Descriptively the drops differ by category, but a proper test (see Statistics below)
shows the **category × temperature interaction is NOT significant** for 4 of 5 models —
categories degrade roughly *proportionally*. The safe claim is two strong main effects
(category difficulty; temperature degradation), not an interaction.

## Table 8 — Scale effects within family (t=0.0)

| Family | 7B | 14B | 32B |
|---|---|---|---|
| OLMo-3 | 46.18 | — | 58.64 (+12.5 pp vs 7B) |
| Qwen2.5 | 67.96 | 68.48 (+0.5) | 73.40 (+4.9 vs 14B) |

**Notable:** on the focused balanced set, Qwen2.5 **7B → 14B gains only +0.5 pp** —
scaling is not uniformly beneficial. (On the full 12,723-question set the same step is
larger, so this is a balanced-subset finding worth flagging.)

## Table 9 — Error distribution by category (t=0.0, run 1)

Errors concentrate in *Clinical Findings* for every model (highest within-category
error rate); see `tables/table9_error_distribution.csv` for full counts and shares.

- **fig3** — Per-category accuracy heatmap at t=0.0.
- **fig4** — Answer instability vs temperature (line plot per model).

## Statistical analysis (`analysis/compute_stats.py`)

### Wilson 95% confidence intervals (overall, t=0.0)
| Model | Acc | 95% CI |
|---|---|---|
| OLMo-3 7B | 46.12 | [43.09, 49.17] |
| OLMo-3 32B | 58.64 | [55.61, 61.61] |
| Qwen2.5 7B | 67.96 | [65.05, 70.74] |
| Qwen2.5 14B | 68.45 | [65.54, 71.21] |
| Qwen2.5 32B | 73.40 | [70.62, 76.01] |

Qwen2.5 **7B and 14B CIs overlap heavily** → not separable on this set.

### Exact McNemar tests (paired, t=0.0)
| Comparison | p-value | Significant? |
|---|---|---|
| OLMo-3 7B vs Qwen2.5 7B | 7.9e-35 | yes |
| OLMo-3 32B vs Qwen2.5 32B | 1.0e-19 | yes |
| **Qwen2.5 7B vs 14B** | **0.18** | **no** |
| Qwen2.5 14B vs 32B | 2.8e-04 | yes |
| OLMo-3 7B vs 32B | 6.2e-13 | yes |
| **Qwen2.5 7B vs OLMo-3 32B** | **5.9e-09** | **yes (smaller Qwen beats larger OLMo)** |

Two robust, paper-ready claims: (a) the **Qwen2.5 7B→14B gain is not statistically
significant**; (b) **Qwen2.5 7B significantly outperforms the 4× larger OLMo-3 32B** —
architecture/pretraining beats scale across families here.

### Category × temperature interaction (logistic-regression LR test)
Not significant for OLMo-3 7B/32B, Qwen2.5 7B/32B (p = 0.06–0.96); significant only for
Qwen2.5 14B (p=0.02). **Conclusion: no consistent interaction** — report category
difficulty and temperature degradation as independent main effects.

### Letter/positional bias (predicted vs gold, t=0.0)
No model shows a statistically significant deviation from the gold letter distribution
(all p > 0.11). Models are **not** positionally biased — a clean negative control.

### Stability as a confidence signal (t=0.7)
Questions where all 3 stochastic runs agree ("stable") are far more likely correct:

| Model | Stable acc | Unstable acc | Gap |
|---|---|---|---|
| OLMo-3 7B | 58.7 | 35.8 | +22.9 |
| OLMo-3 32B | 71.7 | 37.3 | +34.4 |
| Qwen2.5 7B | 80.0 | 39.7 | +40.3 |
| Qwen2.5 14B | 81.5 | 39.3 | +42.2 |
| Qwen2.5 32B | 82.1 | 42.4 | +39.7 |

**Cross-run stability is a strong, logit-free confidence proxy** (gap up to +42 pp) —
direct empirical motivation for self-consistency / verification methods in later papers.

### Temperature rescue vs loss
Going from greedy to majority-vote-at-t=0.7 rescues 32–90 questions but loses more
(80–124); **net effect is negative for every model** (−1.5 to −6.9 pp). Sampling is not
free signal, but the non-trivial rescue set shows greedy is not strictly dominant per-item.

### Cross-model error structure (t=0.0)
Of 1,030 questions: **328 (32%)** solved by all 5 models, **144 (14%) missed by all 5**
(the "hard core"). The hard core skews toward *Clinical Findings* (41 of 144), reinforcing
that category as the systematic weak point rather than a single-model artifact.

### Data quality
**Zero malformed predictions** across all 46,350 model outputs (every prediction is a
valid A–D letter) — answer extraction is fully reliable; no extraction-failure caveat needed.

---

## Section B additions (`analysis/compute_sectionB.py`)

### Effect sizes with paired bootstrap CIs + Holm correction (t=0.0)
| Comparison | Δ acc (pp) | 95% bootstrap CI | Holm-adj p | Sig? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Qwen2.5 7B − OLMo-3 7B | +21.84 | [18.45, 25.15] | 4.8e-34 | yes |
| Qwen2.5 32B − OLMo-3 32B | +14.76 | [11.65, 17.86] | 5.1e-19 | yes |
| **Qwen2.5 14B − 7B** | **+0.49** | **[−0.10, 1.07]** | 0.18 | **no (CI spans 0)** |
| Qwen2.5 32B − 14B | +4.95 | [2.33, 7.57] | 5.5e-04 | yes |
| OLMo-3 32B − 7B | +12.52 | [9.22, 15.92] | 2.5e-12 | yes |
| Qwen2.5 7B − OLMo-3 32B | +9.32 | [6.31, 12.43] | 1.8e-08 | yes |

The Qwen 7B→14B null result survives multiple-comparison correction and its effect-size
CI includes zero — a defensible "scale did not help here" claim.

### Token / generation-length analysis — a real methodological finding
Base models differ enormously in stopping behavior:
- **Qwen2.5 7B/14B hit the 800-token cap on ~75% of questions at t=0.0** — they do not
  emit a stop token and keep generating (often fabricating a *new* question after
  answering). Accuracy is unharmed because the answer letter appears first and extraction
  takes the leading choice, but this is an important caveat for the methods section.
- **OLMo-3 base models stop almost immediately** (mean ~4 tokens for 7B at t=0.0).
- Across models, **wrong answers tend to have slightly longer generations** than correct
  ones; truncation-at-cap falls as temperature rises (more varied stopping).

This justifies the "answer = first valid letter" extraction rule and explains why greedy
decoding is reliable despite base-model verbosity. See `tables/sectionB_token_analysis.csv`.

### Within-model temperature drop with bootstrap CIs (t=0.0 − t=0.7, run1)
OLMo-3 7B +2.91 pp [0.00, 5.83] (weakest, borderline); OLMo-3 32B +7.57 [4.76, 10.39];
Qwen2.5 7B +10.19 [7.38, 13.01]; 14B +9.42 [6.70, 12.23]; 32B +9.03 [6.70, 11.46].
All but OLMo-3 7B are clearly significant.

### Full-set vs focused-set reconciliation
| Model | Full (12,723, 5-opt) | Focused (1,030, 4-opt) | Δ |
|---|---|---|---|
| OLMo-3 7B | 39.86 | 46.18 | +6.32 |
| OLMo-3 32B | 52.12 | 58.64 | +6.52 |
| Qwen2.5 7B | 53.70 | 67.96 | **+14.26** |
| Qwen2.5 14B | 60.88 | 68.48 | +7.60 |
| Qwen2.5 32B | 67.01 | 73.40 | +6.39 |

**Benchmark construction changes the scaling story:** Qwen2.5 7B→14B is **+7.18 pp on the
full set but only +0.52 pp on the focused balanced set**. The 4-option balanced subset
compresses the small/mid Qwen gap (and Qwen 7B gains most from the format, +14 pp). This is
itself a finding about how aggregate vs balanced evaluation can mislead scaling conclusions.

### Qualitative error samples
Hard-core (all-5-wrong) items per category written to `error_samples.md`: Clinical
Findings 41, Diagnosis 30, Next Step 30, Mechanism 22, Treatment 21 — ready to slot into
the Error Analysis section.

---

## Headline findings for the paper
1. **Aggregate accuracy masks category structure** — Clinical Findings is uniformly
   hardest (gap of ~10–13 pp below the easiest category within each model).
2. **Temperature monotonically degrades accuracy** (5–10 pp from t=0.0→t=0.7) and
   inflates answer instability to 45–68% of questions; effect is category-dependent.
3. **Greedy decoding is essentially deterministic** here (std ≤ 0.05 pp) — strong
   reproducibility baseline.
4. **Self-consistency (majority vote) recovers only ~3–4 pp** at t=0.7, not the full gap.
5. **Scale helps unevenly** — large within-OLMo and 14B→32B Qwen gains, but a
   negligible Qwen 7B→14B step on the balanced subset.

6. **Stronger pretraining beats scale across families** — Qwen2.5 7B significantly
   outperforms OLMo-3 32B (McNemar p≈6e-9); within-Qwen 7B→14B is not significant.
7. **Stability is a usable confidence signal** (+23 to +42 pp accuracy gap), and a
   14%-of-set "hard core" is missed by every model — both motivate verification work.

## Verification log
- Independent recompute of Qwen2.5-32B t=0.0 run1: 73.398% via both `predicted==correct`
  and the stored `is_correct` flag (match).
- Every result file asserted to contain exactly 1,030 records with 206 per category.
- Category accuracies sum consistently; predicted-letter distribution well-formed (A–D).
